paragraphs.text: has been looking for the home of the "
," the inspiration for Scott O’Dell’s 1960 Newbery Medal-winning novel The Island of the Blue Dolphins. The book is based on the abandonment and retrieval of
may answer the question that has long eluded researchers: "How did she live for those 18 years?"
There are a number of sources that discuss cave sites on
showing evidence of Indian occupation, but only one cave site had been discovered: the "
," which contains pictographs and petroglyphs primarily depicting sea life such as killer whales. There is little evidence of occupation within
and the cave floor is located just above the high-tide line, so during stormy weather waves crash in — making habitation unlikely.
During archaeological surveys conducted in recent decades, no cave site other than the
had been located. This led to the conclusion that if there was a cave where
lived, it had either collapsed or been buried in the intervening decades.
During research into the ranching history of
, a map was located which clarified the question of a second cave site. A
from the summer of 1879 clearly shows the location of "
is near — but definitely distinct from — the
. The map shows a survey station, named "
," using the normal symbol for a survey station, but near this, the annotation "
" — without the survey station symbol — appears to denote a specific place name. This map established the clear location of a second cave and erased all notions of
being separate references to the same geographic feature.
During the next 20 years, various attempts were made to locate

. The map, while detailed, did not aid in the search because of the complex nature of the shoreline and the scale of the map. Surface surveys and auger and shovel sampling were conducted at all four locations with no success.
"It was not possible to locate one specific spot as the location of the
said. "There were at least four topographic features in the immediate vicinity that could be evidence of a collapsed or buried cave."
A recently obtained copy of the field notes which accompany the 1879 map from
, provided a more specific location for
and precise distance and bearing from the cave survey station to the
With this very specific location and some help from California State University, Los Angeles (
was finally located in early 2012. This involved clearing vegetation and removing recently deposited sediments that were filling
. Excavations proceeded down to where a rounded sandstone boulder was found, likely indicating the floor of

. At that point, work by the team stopped so as not to impact the deposit or begin excavation without proper approvals. As the site was being cleaned up for photographs, a glass bottle was recovered from the bottom of the cleared area. The bottle is a Gothic-style condiment bottle that dates between 1840 and 1865. Such a date would be consistent with the cave’s use by
and the team to ask the question, "So what did she do for 18 years?" The team hopes
will provide an answer once work resumes.
"It’s very rare in archaeology to be able to pinpoint any find to a specific individual,"
said. "To have such a narrow time period that you’re looking at is exactly what we need to pinpoint the context of these finds. It’s just something you never see around here."
" due to the fact that its location was lost for many years. Once it can be firmly established that it was the home of the
Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island

. About nine miles long and three miles wide,
since 1933 as a weapons testing and training facility.

is critical to performing the Sea Range mission and provides a cornerstone for supporting a wide variety of test, evaluation, training and experimentation operations. In conjunction with Navy Region Southwest’s environmental team, the
, which includes archaeologists, ensures that testing can be conducted without compromising any of the island’s flora, fauna, or historical resources.

for testing in support of the warfighter. Having read the book in grade school and personally witnessed the valuable history being discovered throughout the years on
, I am inspired by the integrity of our folks working on this project and their continued perseverance in piecing together the true story of
